query: is low serum chloride a risk factor for cardiovascular mortality

retrieved_docs.title: Ketoacidosis (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Aggressive volume resuscitation with isotonic saline infusion is recommended in the initial management of DKA. Volume expansion not only corrects the hemodynamic instability but also improves insulin sensitivity and reduces counter-regulatory hormone levels. After starting with isotonic saline, the subsequent options can be decided on the serum sodium levels that are corrected for the level of hyperglycemia. Normal or high serum sodium levels warrant replacement with hypotonic saline, and low sodium levels warrant continuation of the isotonic saline. This fluid has to be supplemented with dextrose once the level reaches around 200 to 250 mg/dl. Along with fluids, an intravenous infusion of regular insulin has to be initiated to maintain the blood glucose level between 150 to 200 mg/dl and until the high anion-gap acidosis is resolved in DKA. Like mentioned above, potassium levels are usually high because of the transcellular shifts due to the acidosis and the lack of insulin. When the potassium levels are low, this means that the total body potassium is low. Hence, insulin therapy should be postponed until at least the level of serum potassium is greater than 3.3 mEq/L. Otherwise, a further drop in levels would put the patient at risk for cardiac arrhythmias. In the 3.3 to 5 mEq/L; range, supplementation should be added in the maintenance fluids to target a steady 4.0 to 5.0 mEq/L range, and if higher than that, insulin and intravenous fluids alone can be started with just the frequent monitoring of the serum potassium level. The treatment of the acidosis itself is more controversial. Treatment with sodium bicarbonate therapy is controversial. It has been studied and found to provide no added benefit when the arterial blood pH is greater than 6.9 and may be associated with more harm. [5] A 2011 systematic review found that bicarbonate administration worsened ketonemia. Several studies have found higher potassium requirements in patients receiving bicarbonate. Studies in children have observed a possible association between bicarbonate therapy and cerebral edema. [6]

retrieved_docs.content: Aggressive volume resuscitation with isotonic saline infusion is recommended in the initial management of DKA. Volume expansion not only corrects the hemodynamic instability but also improves insulin sensitivity and reduces counter-regulatory hormone levels. After starting with isotonic saline, the subsequent options can be decided on the serum sodium levels that are corrected for the level of hyperglycemia. Normal or high serum sodium levels warrant replacement with hypotonic saline, and low sodium levels warrant continuation of the isotonic saline. This fluid has to be supplemented with dextrose once the level reaches around 200 to 250 mg/dl. Along with fluids, an intravenous infusion of regular insulin has to be initiated to maintain the blood glucose level between 150 to 200 mg/dl and until the high anion-gap acidosis is resolved in DKA. Like mentioned above, potassium levels are usually high because of the transcellular shifts due to the acidosis and the lack of insulin. When the potassium levels are low, this means that the total body potassium is low, and hence, insulin therapy should be postponed till at least the level of serum potassium is greater than 3.3 mEq/L. Otherwise, a further drop in levels would put the patient at risk for cardiac arrhythmias. In the 3.3 to 5 mEq/L; range, supplementation should be added in the maintenance fluids to target a steady 4.0 to 5.0 mEq/L range, and if higher than that, insulin and intravenous fluids alone can be started with just the frequent monitoring of the serum potassium level. The treatment of the acidosis itself is more controversial. Treatment with sodium bicarbonate therapy is controversial. It has been studied and found to provide no added benefit when the arterial blood pH is greater than 6.9 and may be associated with more harm. [7] A 2011 systematic review found that bicarbonate administration worsened ketonemia. Several studies have found higher potassium requirements in patients receiving bicarbonate. Studies in children have observed a possible association between bicarbonate therapy and cerebral edema. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypovolemia and Hypovolemic Shock -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: While hypovolemia is primarily a clinical diagnosis, clinicians can confirm the diagnosis with a low urinary sodium concentration. [16] With hypovolemia, the kidneys conserve sodium and water to expand the extracellular volume. The urine sodium concentration should be less than 20 meq/L in patients with hypovolemia. Exceptions to this finding include patients with salt-wasting states, such as those on diuretics or with underlying kidney disease, kidney ischemia associated with acute glomerulonephritis or bilateral renal artery stenosis, or those on a very low-sodium diet. Additional scenarios where the urine sodium may exceed 20 meq/L despite volume depletion are a high rate of water resorption and sodium excretion with another anion, as in metabolic alkalosis. Urine sodium can also be low in patients who are euvolemic with heart failure, cirrhosis, or nephrotic syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypermagnesemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypermagnesemia is an uncommon electrolyte disorder. It occurs in approximately 10 to 15% of hospitalized patients with renal failure. [12] Furthermore, epidemiological data suggest that there is a significant prevalence of high levels of serum magnesium in selected healthy populations. For instance, Syedmoradi et al. demonstrated that the overall prevalence of hypermagnesemia was 3.0%, especially in males (p < 0.05) in Iranian subjects. [13] It could be interesting to evaluate hypermagnesemia as a risk factor for other diseases. For instance, Cheungpasitporn et al. found that high magnesium concentrations were typical in people with cardiovascular disease, and 2.3 mg/dL or higher values were associated with worse hospital mortality. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Dietary Lifestyle Modification in Chronic Disease Prevention and Management -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium and potassium are essential in regulating blood pressure and kidney function, with significant implications for cardiovascular and renal health. Excessive sodium intake, which is common in Western diets, is strongly associated with elevated blood pressure. Reducing dietary sodium to recommended levels (<2 g/d) can lower both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, with particularly notable effects observed in individuals with hypertension and across genetically diverse populations. [4] [5] However, the impact of sodium reduction on lipid profiles remains debated, as studies show mixed results regarding its influence on cholesterol and triglyceride levels. [6] Additionally, high sodium intake is associated with an increased risk of CKD and worsening proteinuria. In contrast, sodium restriction has demonstrated benefits in patients with CKD and diabetic nephropathy by reducing urinary albumin excretion and slowing disease progression. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Protection -- Technique or Treatment -- Warm vs Cold Cardioplegia
retrieved_docs.content: Proponents of cold blood cardioplegia later countered these concerns by showing that hypothermia does not adversely affect hemoglobin's oxygen delivery capabilities. This is because myocardial acidosis and hypercarbia during arrest counteract the leftward shift of the oxygen dissociation curve caused by hypothermia and, in some cases, even shift it to the right. [15] The other detrimental effects of hypothermia were argued to be theoretical, as natural additives in the blood—such as magnesium, buffers, and plasma proteins—help counteract calcium sequestration, hypercarbic acidosis, and cellular edema, respectively. Furthermore, close control of hematocrit during CPB using hemofiltration limits sludging and rouleaux formation. [16] The addition of procaine helps counteract membrane instability, mitigating potential damage. While no significant harm is associated with cold blood cardioplegia, study results have shown its benefits, particularly in restoring baseline myocardial functions after prolonged aortic cross-clamping. [17] [18] As a result, multiple surveys have indicated that most surgeons in the UK and US now prefer cold blood cardioplegia over warm blood cardioplegia.

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A study examined the correlation between serum selenium concentrations and the outcomes of patients with heart failure within a Chinese population. The findings revealed a notable impact on survival rates, particularly in individuals with reduced serum selenium levels. Patients in the bottom quartile, characterized by the lowest serum selenium concentration, exhibited the worst prognosis among heart failure patients, showcasing a significantly heightened risk of all-cause mortality. These findings emphasize that selenium levels are crucial in determining prognosis. Interestingly, no discernible association was noted between serum selenium concentration and incidences of rehospitalization among these patients with heart failure, pointing to the multifactorial etiology of Keshan disease. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Systolic Hypertension -- Evaluation -- Routine Laboratory and Clinical Investigation
retrieved_docs.content: These tests should be performed to evaluate cardiovascular risk and concomitant diseases. 12-lead electrocardiographic (left ventricular hypertrophy features, atrial dilation, and arrhythmias) Lipid panels (calculate 10-year atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk) Serum creatinine (with an estimation of glomerular filtration rate) Serum electrolytes (sodium and potassium) Thyroid-stimulating hormone Urine analysis (proteinuria) Urine microalbuminuria

retrieved_docs.title: Hypovolemia and Hypovolemic Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Current guidelines recommend crystalloid fluids over colloid solutions for resuscitating patients with severe volume depletion not caused by hemorrhage. Healthcare professionals tailor the choice of crystalloid solution to the individual patient. Isotonic saline contains a higher chloride concentration than plasma and may cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis when administered in large volumes. LR is also isotonic but has a lower chloride content. [24] Evidence suggests that using balanced crystalloids, such as LR solution, in large-volume resuscitation may reduce the risk of renal injury, particularly when a restrictive chloride strategy is employed. However, the lactate in the LR solution converts to bicarbonate and can worsen metabolic alkalosis. Crystalloids are equally effective and significantly less costly than colloid solutions. Commonly used colloids include albumin and hyperoncotic starch, though studies have not shown improved outcomes with albumin. Clinicians should avoid hyperoncotic starch, as studies associate it with increased risks of renal failure and mortality. [25] For a detailed comparison of fluid types and their clinical indications, please see StatPearls' companion topic, " Fluid Management ."

retrieved_docs.title: Hypokalemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hypokalemia is a common electrolyte disturbance not given as much attention as hyperkalemia. Hypokalemia has associations with cardiac disease, renal failure, malnutrition, and shock. Additional risk factors, such as hypothermia and increased blood cell production, contribute to this condition's complexity. Clinicians must thus promptly triage, refer, and replete electrolytes to prevent long-term sequelae and mortality. Understanding the nuances of the condition's manifestations and treatment strategies is crucial to enhancing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Etiology -- Increased Potassium Intake
retrieved_docs.content: Increased potassium intake from food is a very uncommon cause of hyperkalemia in adult patients with normal renal function but can be an important cause in those with kidney disease. Foods with high potassium content include dried fruits, seaweed, nuts, molasses, avocados, and Lima beans. Many vegetables that are also high in potassium include spinach, potatoes, tomatoes, broccoli, beets, carrots, and squash. High-potassium-containing fruits include kiwis, mangoes, oranges, bananas, and cantaloupe. Red meats are also rich in potassium. While generally safe to consume even in large quantities by patients with normal potassium homeostasis, these foods should be avoided in patients with severe renal disease or other underlying conditions or medications predisposing them to hyperkalemia. Intravenous intake through high potassium-containing fluids, particularly total parenteral nutrition, medications with high potassium content, and massive blood transfusions can significantly elevate serum potassium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Goldman Risk Indices (Archived) -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Goldman Risk Index Scoring
retrieved_docs.content: The original GRI identifies a risk index class based on the presence or absence of nine preoperative criteria potentially associated with postoperative cardiac complications. These factors are: Age over 70 years (5 points) Myocardial infarction occurring within the last 6 months (10 points) Presence of heart failure signs (jugular vein distention, JVD, or ventricular gallop) (11 points) Significant aortic stenosis (3 points) Arrhythmia (other than sinus or premature atrial contractions) (7 points) The presence of 5 or more premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) per minute (7 points) Medical history or conditions including the presence of PO2 less than 60; PCO2 greater than 50; K below 3; HCO3 under 20; BUN over 50; serum creatinine greater than 3; elevated SGOT; chronic liver disease; or the state of being bedridden (3 points) Type of operation: emergency (4 points); intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, or aortic (3 points)

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Heart Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory testing is recommended for all patients with HHD to assess underlying causes and associated cardiovascular risk factors. Recommended studies include fasting blood glucose, complete blood count, lipid profile, serum creatinine with estimated glomerular filtration rate, serum sodium, potassium, calcium, blood urea nitrogen, and thyroid-stimulating hormone, particularly in the context of atrial fibrillation. A urine analysis, including urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, should be considered for assessing renal involvement. Additional laboratory studies may be required to evaluate for secondary causes of hypertension if clinically suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypokalemic Metabolic Alkalosis -- Etiology -- Low Blood Pressure
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic diseases Bartter syndrome Gitelman syndrome Autosomal dominant hypocalcemia with hypercalciuria (ADHH) Acquired diseases Diuretic use Vomiting

retrieved_docs.title: Crystalloid Fluids -- Adverse Effects -- Normal Saline (0.9% Saline)
retrieved_docs.content: Normal saline has a higher concentration of chloride ions (154 mmol/L) than human serum (98 to 106 mmol/L). With the administration of large volumes of normal saline, hyperchloremia occurs. [9] While there is still some debate on the exact mechanism of this pH disturbance, the thinking is that the increase in chloride concentration adjusts the substantial ion difference in plasma, resulting in more free water in the intravascular space. As a result, the hydrogen ion concentration in the serum would increase to maintain electrochemical neutrality. Excessive renal bicarbonate excretion can occur, resulting in metabolic acidosis. [10] The dilution of serum bicarbonate through non-buffered crystalloids (eg, normal saline) may also contribute to acidosis. Besides metabolic acidosis, clinical research has shown that high volumes of normal saline can cause hyperchloremia-induced renal afferent arteriole constriction, which can cause a decrease in the glomerular filtration rate. [11] [9] Hypertonic sodium chloride solutions can cause vein damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Protection -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance Cardioplegic solutions must be carefully balanced to avoid electrolyte imbalances. Excessive potassium levels can affect myocardial depolarization and repolarization, while imbalances in other electrolytes like calcium and sodium can disrupt normal cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1820
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) is a disorder characterized by abnormal concentrations of key ions in the blood, and it encompasses several specific clinical entities. One component of this disorder is potassium deficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), which reflects low serum potassium levels and can lead to muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, and metabolic disturbances. Another manifestation is an increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية), indicating the presence of unmeasured anions such as lactate or ketoacids and often signaling metabolic acidosis. Hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), the elevation of serum uric acid, may also arise within the context of electrolyte disturbances and is associated with gout, kidney stones, and renal impairment. Finally, low blood chloride (نقص كلوريد الدم) represents hypochloremia, a condition that can exacerbate metabolic alkalosis and affect fluid balance. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which electrolyte homeostasis can be disrupted, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and correction of electrolyte abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2776
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý, a calcium chloride preparation, is contraindicated in patients with رَجَفانٌ بطيني (ventricular fibrillation) and in those who have فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia), because administration of calcium chloride in these conditions can exacerbate cardiac arrhythmia or further increase already elevated serum calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance is a broader disorder that includes both hyperuricemia and hypochloremia as specific manifestations, indicating that disturbances in electrolyte homeostasis can present as elevated uric acid levels in the blood as well as reduced chloride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) is associated with serious cardiac complications, including توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), and its use is contraindicated in the presence of فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia). Recognizing that calcium chloride can precipitate life‑threatening arrhythmias and that hyper‑calcemia represents a condition in which calcium chloride should not be administered is essential for safe patient management and for preventing adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) is identified as a contraindicated agent in several serious clinical situations. Its administration should be avoided in patients who are experiencing cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, as the drug is considered contraindicated in the context of these life‑threatening arrhythmias. In addition, chlorid vápenatý is contraindicated in the presence of فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia), because giving additional calcium could exacerbate the already elevated serum calcium levels. These contraindications underscore the importance of careful assessment of cardiac rhythm disturbances and calcium metabolism status before considering calcium chloride therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2815
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance encompasses several related disturbances, including an increased anion gap, hyperuricemia, and hypochloremia. An increased anion gap reflects a shift in the balance of plasma ions that can signal underlying metabolic derangements. Hyperuricemia denotes elevated levels of uric acid in the blood, which may contribute to or result from altered electrolyte homeostasis. Hypochloremia indicates a deficiency of chloride in the bloodstream, further illustrating the spectrum of abnormalities that fall under the broader category of electrolyte imbalance. Recognizing these interconnected components is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients with electrolyte disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1589
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated both with hypercholesterolemia and with chloride deficiency (نقص كلوريد الدم). These relationships indicate that patients with type 2 diabetes often have elevated cholesterol levels, which can increase the risk of developing acute pancreatitis, and that the inflammatory episode of acute pancreatitis may be accompanied by electrolyte disturbances such as low serum chloride. Recognizing the links among type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, acute pancreatitis, and chloride deficiency supports more comprehensive evaluation and integrated management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Hypernatremia, defined as an elevated concentration of sodium in the blood, is classified within the broader group of nutritional and metabolic diseases, reflecting its nature as a disorder of fluid and electrolyte balance that falls under the umbrella of metabolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: High plasma osmolality, high blood glucose, acute kidney failure, type 2 diabetes, an abnormal psychological state, and low blood volume are closely interrelated clinical entities. High plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels, reflecting the hyperosmolar consequences of uncontrolled diabetes, and it is also linked to type 2 diabetes itself, indicating that patients with this form of diabetes frequently develop increased plasma osmolality. In addition, high plasma osmolality is clinically associated with an abnormal psychological state, consistent with the neurocognitive disturbances that can arise from severe hyperosmolar conditions, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that dehydration or hypovolemia often accompanies the hyperosmolar state. Acute kidney failure is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes, underscoring diabetes as a major risk factor for rapid loss of renal function, and it is also linked to an abnormal psychological state, reflecting the mental status changes that can result from uremic toxins. Moreover, acute kidney failure is clinically associated with low blood volume, indicating that hypovolemia can precipitate or worsen renal injury. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which type 2 diabetes can lead to high plasma osmolality and acute kidney failure, both of which are connected to low blood volume and neuropsychiatric disturbances, emphasizing the importance of monitoring osmolar status, volume balance, renal function, and mental health in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is a specific manifestation of fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base disorders, classified under hyperosmolarity and hypernatremia within the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte and acid‑base balance (states of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance disorder). It is inversely related to the Arabic concept اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات (electrolyte imbalance) and maps to ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (plasma hyperosmolality). The electrolyte‑imbalance group includes hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), potassium deficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية), hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), hyponatremia syndrome (متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم), and hypochloremia (نقص كلوريد الدم). Acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically linked to both hyper‑ and hypokalemia, hypovolemia (low blood‑volume state), hyponatremia syndrome, potassium deficiency syndrome, type 2 diabetes mellitus requiring insulin,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated with hypochloremia (نقص كلوريد الدم), meaning that patients experiencing an acute inflammatory attack of the pancreas often exhibit low serum chloride levels, which can reflect the fluid and electrolyte disturbances commonly seen in this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 190
retrieved_docs.content: serum chloride, and may be accompanied by extrarenal nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية). Hyperuricemia and hyperkalemia further increase the risk of acute kidney injury, which is also related to low intravascular volume, hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) is a subcategory of acute kidney injury and contributes to plasma hyperosmolality; pyelonephritis (التهاب الحويضة والكلية) is associated with hyponatremia. Alcoholic liver inflammation (الْتِهابُ الكَبِدِ الكحوليّ) and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) are additional comorbidities that intersect with fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base imbalances, the latter often co‑occurring with hypokalemia. Cystic fibrosis (تليف كيسي) and its variant without meconium ileus are linked to the same electrolyte and acid‑base disturbances, and amiloride hydrochloride (أميلوريد هيدروكلوريد) is noted as a therapeutic option.
